confidence.disclosure: Sayyid Jamal al-Din 'al-Afghani' (more probably born in Asadabad in Iran than in Afghanistan, despite the nisba he himself promoted) is one of the most important and most contested figures of modern Islamic political thought. His own statements about his origins are deliberately misleading, and the early part of his life is shrouded in obscurity, but the major events from the 1860s onward - his presence in Kabul, his expulsion to India, his years in Cairo, the publication of al-Urwa al-Wuthqa from Paris in 1884, his summons to Iran by Nasir al-Din Shah, his expulsion, his role in the Tobacco Protest of 1891, and his death in Istanbul in 1897 - are firm. The dates and places of his early travels are reconstructed from contemporary diplomatic sources.

sources: Nikki Keddie, An Islamic Response to Imperialism (1968)
Nikki Keddie, Sayyid Jamal ad-Din al-Afghani: A Political Biography (1972)
Albert Hourani, Arabic Thought in the Liberal Age (1962)
al-Urwa al-Wuthqa (the journal he co-edited with Muhammad Abduh)

journey.event: Born in 1838 (or by some accounts 1839) in Asadabad in northwestern Iran, near Hamadan, into a Twelver Shia sayyid family - despite his later insistence that he was born in As'adabad in Afghanistan and was a Sunni. The Iranian origin and Shia background are firmly established by Iranian documents. His youthful adoption of the Afghan identity is a calculated political choice.

Receives his early religious education in Qazvin and Tehran, and then more advanced training in the great Shia centres of Najaf and Karbala in Iraq, where he studies the rationalist Shia tradition (most importantly the school of Shaykh Murtada Ansari and the early influences of Shaykhi philosophy).

Around 1857 (the year of the Indian Revolt against the British) is in India, possibly Bombay. Witnesses the trauma of the Mutiny and its bloody suppression. The experience deepens his hostility to British imperialism and turns into a lifelong concern with the political weakness of the Muslim world before European power.

By 1866 is in Kabul at the court of the amir Muhammad A'zam Khan, one of the contestants for the Afghan throne after the death of Dost Muhammad. Serves as a confidential adviser to the amir. It is during these years that he begins to call himself 'al-Afghani' and to claim Afghan birth.

When Sher Ali Khan defeats Muhammad A'zam Khan and takes the Afghan throne in 1868, the new amir is suspicious of al-Afghani's anti-British sentiments and expels him. He travels via India - where the British keep him under surveillance and force him to leave Bombay quickly - to Cairo and then to Istanbul.

By 1870 is in Istanbul, where the reformist Tanzimat government welcomes him initially. He gives a famous and controversial public lecture at the inauguration of the new Istanbul university (Dar ül-Fünun) in 1870 in which he describes prophecy as a kind of human craft. The traditionalist religious establishment is outraged, the lecture is condemned as heretical, and al-Afghani is expelled from the city.

In March 1871 arrives in Cairo. The Egyptian khedive Ismail's government allows him to settle and even gives him a stipend. For the next eight years he lives in Cairo, gathering around him a circle of disciples - the most important of whom is the young Muhammad Abduh - and teaching philosophy, theology, and politics in his own informal way at his home and at al-Azhar.

After the deposition of khedive Ismail by the Ottoman sultan in June 1879 (under European pressure), al-Afghani's political activities (he has been organising opposition to the new ruler and to the European-controlled Egyptian government) become unwelcome. In August 1879 he is summarily expelled from Egypt by the new khedive Tawfiq. He never returns.

Spends two years in India (mostly in Hyderabad and Calcutta), where he composes his famous 'Refutation of the Materialists' (al-Radd ala al-Dahriyyin) in Persian, a polemic against the Indian Muslim modernist Sayyid Ahmad Khan and against the philosophical materialism that al-Afghani sees as a Western threat to Islamic civilisation.

In late 1882, after the British occupation of Egypt following the Urabi revolt, leaves India for Europe. Spends some time in London, where he engages in journalism and political agitation against British policy in the Muslim world.

In Paris in 1884 is joined by his disciple Muhammad Abduh, who has been exiled from Egypt for his role in the Urabi revolt. Together they found and edit the Arabic journal al-Urwa al-Wuthqa ('The Indissoluble Bond'), one of the most influential Arabic political journals of the 19th century. Eighteen issues are produced from March to October 1884 before the journal is suppressed (it is banned in British India and Egypt). The journal becomes the founding text of pan-Islamic political thought.

In 1886, after his Paris journal is suppressed, accepts an invitation from the Iranian shah Nasir al-Din to come to Iran. Travels to Bushire on the Persian Gulf and then north to Tehran. Initially well received, he soon falls out with the shah's government and is expelled in 1887.

Travels to Russia, where he spends most of 1887-1889 in Saint Petersburg and Moscow trying to interest the Russian government in pan-Islamic schemes against Britain. The Russians use him for propaganda but never give him serious support.

Returns to Iran in 1889, again at the shah's invitation - but quickly falls out with the government a second time over the British tobacco concession of 1890, which gives a British firm a monopoly over Iranian tobacco. From the sanctuary of the shrine of Shah Abd al-Azim near Tehran, al-Afghani composes incendiary letters and pamphlets denouncing the concession.

In January 1891 is forcibly removed from the shrine of Shah Abd al-Azim by the shah's troops, taken under guard to the Iraqi border, and dumped at Basra. From Basra he writes a famous open letter to the chief Shia mujtahid Mirza Hasan Shirazi in Samarra, urging him to issue a fatwa against the tobacco concession. Shirazi obliges; the Tobacco Protest of 1891-1892 forces the shah to cancel the concession and is a foundational moment in modern Iranian political history.

In 1892 accepts an invitation from Sultan Abdulhamid II to come to Istanbul, where the sultan keeps him as a kind of honoured guest-prisoner in a luxurious house in Nisantasi. The sultan hopes to use him in his pan-Islamic propaganda; al-Afghani hopes to influence the sultan's policy. Neither is fully satisfied.

In May 1896 the Iranian shah Nasir al-Din is assassinated in the shrine of Shah Abd al-Azim by Mirza Reza Kermani, a follower of al-Afghani. The Iranian government demands his extradition; the Ottoman sultan refuses to surrender his guest. The episode marks the high point of al-Afghani's reputation as a revolutionary force in the Muslim world.

Dies in Istanbul on 9 March 1897, aged 59, of cancer of the jaw (though some have suggested poisoning by the Ottoman authorities, as the timing was convenient for them). He is buried in the Sheikler Mezarligi cemetery in Istanbul. In 1944 his remains are repatriated to Kabul at the request of the Afghan government and reinterred at Kabul University, where his tomb has stood ever since. His ideas - pan-Islamic political solidarity, modernist religious reform, militant anti-imperialism - shape every major current of 20th-century Islamic political thought.
